customer_anxiety_profile: Rong is a careful, deliberate homeowner — 20-year tenure, pride in the home, emotionally invested in its condition. His primary anxiety is roof damage during installation; it surfaced twice and was never resolved beyond 'ask the specialist.' He is also financially cautious: retired, two recent loan rejections, credit likely in the 640-680 range, and aware that his score has been impacted. He is not hostile — he is thorough. Approach him with specific, concrete answers, not reassurances.
unresolved_concerns: Roof damage during installation: raised at 03:30 and 05:07, deflected to specialist both times with no substantive response. Closer must open with a clear workmanship warranty explanation before moving forward.

Credit score borderline: Rong self-reports 640-680 bracketing the 670 threshold. He understands why it dropped (two hard pulls during retirement refinance attempts). Closer needs to know ION's policy on stated vs pulled credit and should not assume this is a hard DQ without the actual pull.

Net metering / excess energy production: Rong asked specifically about selling excess energy back to the grid, referencing California programs he's aware of. Randy confirmed programs exist but deferred to the specialist. Closer should open with Northern Virginia Electric's net metering rules as a concrete hook.

Ground-mount panels: Rong wants panels in the backyard where he has all-day sun exposure. ION does not offer ground-mount. Closer should acknowledge this cleanly and redirect: the roof system will be designed to maximize the sun exposure the home already captures.

Roof age (20 years): asphalt shingle, Toll Brothers build, 2005. Crosses the 15-year threshold. Closer should probe replacement plans and be prepared to bridge to ION's in-house roofing if Rong is open to it.
sensitive_topics_raised: Deceased first wife: name still on the title as of the call. Rong states he has a 'cease deed' (likely a certified death certificate deed). Closer should not ask about this unless the ownership documentation creates a qualification issue — Rong mentioned it matter-of-factly but it is a personal loss.

Retirement and loan rejections: Rong is retired and was rejected for refinancing by Bank of America and PNC due to insufficient income. He is aware this hurt his credit. Do not frame solar financing in a way that echoes the loan-rejection experience — emphasize that solar financing is based on credit score, not income.

Adult child financial involvement: Rong's son manages his autopay. Probe at the start of the specialist call whether the son should be present — he may be a co-decision-maker and excluding him is a close-rate risk.
exact_promises_rep_made: Randy promised to text Rong a phone number for Northern Virginia Electric so Rong can call and request 12 months of kWh usage data.
Randy said he would 'touch base tomorrow' after Rong gets the bill information — no specific time was locked.
Randy said he would 'put in the notes' Rong's request to be referred to a ground-mount panel company if ION cannot accommodate it.
closer_should_know: Rong is a genuinely warm, first-time solar lead on a large home with strong sun exposure and real financial motivation — but the callback is structurally fragile: no locked time, homework assigned to the customer, a 20-year roof that needs probing, and a credit range that brackets the threshold. The fastest path to converting this call is: (1) open by resolving the roof warranty concern with specifics, (2) use the net metering aspiration as the close hook — it's the thing Rong is most excited about — and (3) check whether the adult son should be on the call before anything is signed.
